calendar.matters:
Notice Of Hearing
Off Calendar Oct-29-2015 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

PETITIONER SANFORD OXLEY'S Notice Of Hearing is Off calendar. Duplicate entry.

Amended Notice Of Hearing Re: Petition For Writ Of Mandamus
Off Calendar Oct-29-2015 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES - ATTY. ERIN DACAYANAN OF THE LAW OFFICES OF MARSANNE WEESE, TEL. NO. 1-415-565-9600 APPEARING FOR THE PETITIONER;
ATTY. JOHN T. McGLOTHLIN, DEPUTY ATTORNEY GENERAL, TEL. NO. 1-510-622-2224 APPEARING FOR THE RESPONDENT.

RULING - A R G U E D; The Court adopted its tentative ruling. PETITIONER SANFORD OXLEY's Petition For Writ Of Mandamus is denied. Petitioner admitted that on the evening of April 21, 2013, he had at most four drinks and that he had the last drink somewhere between 8 and 9 p.m. (DMV 051.) Furthermore, Petitioner admitted that he drove the vehicle between 9:30 and 10:00 p.m. (DMV 046.) The Department of Motor Vehicle's expert witness, Ms. Wong, provided credible testimony regarding Petitioner's Blood Alcohol Content at the time when Petitioner was driving the vehicle. Wong calculated that at 10:00 p.m. P's BAC would have been .1985 percent, at 9:45 p.m. it would have been .202 percent, and at 9:30 p.m. it would have been .206 percent. (DMV 111.) In calculating the BAC, Ms. Wong relied on relevant data, including test results, absorption and elimination rates, and weight. (DMV 101.) The retrograde extrapolation method used by Ms. Wong to determine Petitioner's BAC has speculative elements, in that it requires the expert to consider "'variables such as weight, or time and content of last meal which may affect the rate at which the alcohol dissipates.'" (People v. Thompson (2006) 38 Cal.4th 811, 826.) However, those issues go to the weight of the testimony, rather than the admissibility of the expert testimony. (Id. at 826, 834.) Petitioner did not submit to contradict the credibility of the method used by Ms. Wong. Although Ms. Wong testified that at the time of the administrative hearing, the San Francisco crime lab did not have a Forensic Alcohol Supervisor, Petitioner did not submit evidence that the lab was without a supervisor at the time of Petitioner's arrest. Nor did Petitioner submit any evidence to overcome the statutory presumption that official duty was regularly performed during the administration of the breath tests. (Evid. Code section 664.) Thus, the court properly considered the breath tests in reaching the conclusion that Petitioner drove a vehicle with a blood alcohol content of .08 percent or more. Furthermore, Petitioner's request for attorney fees is denied. The Department of Motor Vehicle's actions in suspending Petitioner's license were not arbitrary and capricious. (Govt. Code section 800.). Order signed in open court. Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter : Melanie Gheno, CSR # 7489 =302/ehg
